did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper introduces PanDerm, a multimodal vision foundation model using deep learning (self-supervised learning on a ViT-large encoder with CLIP-based objectives) to perform dermatology tasks including skin cancer screening, risk stratification, differential diagnosis, lesion segmentation, and metastasis prediction.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work introducing 'PanDerm', a new multimodal vision foundation model. It details the development of the model using self-supervised learning on over 2 million images, evaluates it on 28 diverse benchmarks, and reports results from three reader studies assessing clinical utility. The structure includes standard research sections such as Methods, Results, and Discussion, and it reports new quantitative findings (e.g., AUROC, F1 scores, p-values) rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: A multimodal vision foundation model for clinical dermatology
brief_summary: This study introduces PanDerm, a multimodal vision foundation model pretrained via self-supervised learning on over 2 million real-world skin disease images from 11 clinical institutions across four imaging modalities. The model was evaluated on 28 diverse benchmarks covering skin cancer screening, risk stratification, differential diagnosis, lesion segmentation, and metastasis prediction, achieving state-of-the-art performance often with only 10% of labeled data. Clinical utility was assessed through three reader studies involving human-AI collaboration. PanDerm outperformed clinicians by 10.2% in early-stage melanoma detection via longitudinal analysis and improved diagnostic accuracy by 11% for clinicians and 16.5% for non-dermatologist providers across 128 skin conditions. These results demonstrate PanDerm's potential to enhance patient care in diverse clinical scenarios and serve as a model for multimodal foundation models in other medical specialties.

lesion_types_studied.details: Studied across multiple tasks including early-stage detection, screening, risk stratification, and slide classification. Included in HAM10000, BCN20000, PAD-UFES-20, DDI, Derm7pt, ISIC2024, MSKCC, HIBA, and CPTAC-CM datasets.
Included in HAM10000, BCN20000, PAD-UFES-20, MSKCC, and SD-128 datasets. Used as a comparator to melanoma in binary classification tasks.

Included in BCN20000 and PAD-UFES-20 datasets.

Included in HAM10000, BCN20000, and MSKCC datasets.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories (e.g., skin cancer diagnosis, differential diagnosis across 128 conditions, benign vs. malignant classification, melanoma detection, metastasis prediction, risk stratification (high/low risk)).
all_task_types.details: The paper evaluates PanDerm on tasks including skin cancer screening, differential diagnosis of common and rare skin conditions, lesion segmentation, longitudinal monitoring, and metastasis prediction. Specific tasks include binary classification (benign vs. malignant), multi-class classification (128 skin conditions), and risk stratification (high vs. low risk for recurrence/metastasis). Performance metrics include AUROC, F1 score, and diagnostic accuracy.

The paper explicitly lists 'lesion segmentation' as one of the evaluated tasks. It compares PanDerm's segmentation performance against MedSAM, noting PanDerm achieves slightly better accuracy (0.5% improvement) without specialized training for segmentation.

all_task_types.target: Continuous risk scores or survival probabilities used for stratification.
all_task_types.details: The paper discusses 'risk stratification' and 'metastasis prognosis'. It uses PanDerm prediction scores to stratify patients into high-risk and low-risk groups for recurrence-free intervals (RFI) and survival analysis (Kaplan-Meier curves, Cox proportional hazards models). While the output is used for binary stratification, the underlying mechanism involves predicting continuous risk scores/probabilities.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To estimate 95% confidence intervals for model performance metrics (e.g., AUROC, W_F1) using 1,000 bootstrap replicates.
To calculate P values for comparing PanDerm's performance against baseline models or other pretrained models.
To assess statistical significance of improvements in reader diagnostic scores and accuracy with versus without PanDerm support, calculated across readers and across classes.
To compare models by implementing 1,000 permutations per pair and recalculating performance.
To evaluate performance on datasets with relatively small sample sizes or following conventional practice, computing mean and standard deviation across folds.
To derive 95% confidence intervals by dividing the standard deviation by the square root of the number of folds (multiplied by 1.96).

hypothesis_testing.test: Two-sided t-test, two-sided paired t-test, pairwise permutation tests, log-rank test
hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no difference in performance (e.g., AUROC, diagnostic accuracy, diagnostic score) between PanDerm and baseline models, or between assisted and unassisted reader performance.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a significant difference in performance between PanDerm and baseline models, or between assisted and unassisted reader performance.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: Statistical significance was determined using p-values. P < 0.05 was considered significant. Specifically, two-sided t-tests were used for comparing model performance on datasets with sufficient sample sizes or for specific task comparisons. Two-sided paired t-tests were used for reader studies to assess improvements in diagnostic scores and accuracy with versus without PanDerm support. Pairwise permutation tests (1,000 permutations) were used for comparing models on remaining datasets. Log-rank tests were used for survival analysis comparisons. P-values reported were < 0.001, < 0.05, or specific values like 0.073, indicating the strength of evidence against the null hypothesis.

pigmented_skin_lesion_datasets.description: Performance comparison on 10 pigmented skin lesion datasets across multiple centers and modalities. Metrics: AUROC for binary class (c=2) and Weighted F1 score (W_F1) for multi-class (c>2).

Impact was more pronounced in the generalist group.

early_stage_melanoma_detection.task: Longitudinal analysis for early-stage melanoma detection

architecture_used.pretraining: Self-supervised learning on over 2 million real-world skin disease images from 11 clinical institutions across 4 imaging modalities (dermatopathology, clinical, dermoscopic, TBP). Continued pretraining from ImageNet-1K weights. Used CLIP-Large as teacher model for representation reconstruction and CLIP latent alignment objectives. Masked image modeling with 50% mask ratio.

architecture_details.backbone: ViT-Large (Vision Transformer Large) with 24 transformer blocks and 1,024 dimensional embeddings
components: Mask regressor (operating similarly to cross-attention, using learnable mask tokens as queries, keys/values from visible patch representations and previous layer outputs)

classification_or_output_head.linear_probing: Logistic regression (linear classifier) applied to top of extracted features
classification_or_output_head.fine_tuning: Encoder fine-tuned with task-specific heads (e.g., risk prediction head, UD detection head, segmentation decoder/head)

preprocessing_techniques: Input resizing to 224 × 224 pixels for the ViT-Large encoder and 196 × 196 pixels for the CLIP-Large teacher model
Masked image modeling with a 50% mask ratio during pretraining
Data augmentation: random resized cropping and horizontal flipping
Sequential data preprocessing for lesion change detection: dark corner removal, skin inpainting, hair removal, image registration, and lesion segmentation

Dark corner removal: conversion to grayscale, contour extraction using OpenCV binary threshold (threshold = 100) and findContours, capturing largest contour via minEnclosingCircle, scaling to 80% radius, and inpainting using the Telea algorithm (radius = 10)
Hair removal: conversion to grayscale, application of a black hat morphological operation with a 17 × 17 structuring element, thresholding to create a binary mask, and inpainting

Image registration: AKAZE feature-based approach with key point detection (descriptor size = 0, threshold = 9 × 10^-5, octaves = 4), Brute Force matcher with Hamming distance, RANSAC for EuclideanTransform estimation, and warping using skimage.transform.warp with reflection padding and linear interpolation
Standardized image quality processing for the default pipeline in short-term lesion change detection

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied during pretraining as a simple data augmentation technique.
Applied during pretraining as a simple data augmentation technique.

conclusions: PanDerm, a multimodal vision foundation model pretrained via self-supervised learning on over 2 million real-world skin disease images from 11 clinical institutions across four imaging modalities, achieved state-of-the-art performance across 28 diverse benchmarks.
PanDerm demonstrated high label efficiency, often outperforming existing models while using only 10% of the labeled data typically required by those models.
In clinical utility assessments, PanDerm outperformed clinicians by 10.2% in early-stage melanoma detection through longitudinal analysis.
PanDerm improved clinicians’ skin cancer diagnostic accuracy by 11% on dermoscopy images.
PanDerm enhanced non-dermatologist healthcare providers’ differential diagnosis accuracy by 16.5% across 128 skin conditions on clinical photographs.

The study concludes that PanDerm has the potential to improve patient care across diverse clinical scenarios and serves as a model for developing multimodal foundation models in other medical specialties.
The integration of AI support via models like PanDerm has the potential to accelerate the adoption of artificial intelligence in healthcare workflows.
important_remarks: Data leakage risk mitigated: The authors explicitly avoided using images from commonly used benchmarks (e.g., ISIC, DermNet) in the pretraining dataset to prevent data leakage, a common flaw in previous studies like SwAVDerm.

Limited disease coverage: The evaluation covered approximately 200 skin conditions, which represents only a fraction of the over 1,000 known dermatological diagnoses, with limited coverage of rare genetic disorders.

Bias assessment limitations: While the model showed stable performance across skin tones without explicitly balanced training data, the authors acknowledge that comprehensive bias assessment requires metrics beyond overall accuracy and that equitable stand-alone performance does not guarantee unbiased human-AI collaboration.

Evaluation scope constraints: The evaluation focused primarily on single imaging modalities and overall accuracy rather than disease-specific analysis, varying disease coverage across anatomical locations, and complex systemic diseases.

Human-AI interaction nuance: PanDerm alone performed equivalently to clinicians with PanDerm assistance in skin cancer diagnosis, suggesting clinicians selectively incorporate AI recommendations rather than adhering blindly, which is crucial for clinical deployment.

Generalizability to other modalities: The model maintained high performance on clinical photographs not used during training, showing AUROC gains of 4.0%, 2.6%, and 2.1% on external clinical datasets.

Label efficiency: PanDerm achieved state-of-the-art performance often using only 10% of the labeled data typically required by existing models, demonstrating strong scalability with limited training data.
